hypothesis: If a 3D molecular encoder is trained via multimodal contrastive alignment with a semantically rich SMILES-based language model, then the resulting 3D representations will exhibit emergent pose-invariance—enabling robust retrieval of molecules across arbitrary rotations without explicit geometric equivariance constraints—because the contrastive objective forces the 3D encoder to learn molecular identity features encoded in the SMILES modality rather than orientation-dependent spatial features.
